explicit_text: My father came to the United States in 1967, seeing it as a land of opportunity where hard work could lead to financial success. I was born in Aleppo, Syria, but moved to the United States in 2000 when I was four years old. Growing up in America has given me perspectives and opportunities that I might not have had in Syria, and I am grateful for that. However, I also understand the challenges faced by Syrian refugees coming to the U.S. today, which is very different from when my family arrived.

explicit_text: I was born in Aleppo, Syria in 1996, and it holds a special place in my heart as my true home. Even after moving to the United States at age four, I spent many summers there, which helped me understand the hardships my family and friends endure. Aleppo is where my roots are, and despite the conflict and difficulties, I dream of returning one day. My father also left Aleppo back in 1967, so it has been central to my family's story for decades.

explicit_text: Syria is my homeland, and no matter where I travel, I always seek a connection to it through memories of family, food, and the neighborhood. My family left Syria in 2000, before the war began, so our experience was very different from those fleeing today. I feel deeply for those still trapped in the conflict and understand why so many are forced to leave. Syria will always be home to me, and I hold onto the hope of returning despite the tragic circumstances my loved ones face now.

explicit_text: When my family moved to the United States in 2000, we traveled legally with visas and flight itineraries, unlike many refugees today who risk dangerous journeys across the Mediterranean Sea. I think about those who have to pile onto rafts and forge documents just to escape conflict, and it makes me realize how fortunate I am. The Mediterranean Sea represents a perilous barrier that many Syrians must cross to find safety, a reality very different from my own family's migration experience.
